title: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

reviews.summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.
reviews.questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.

Questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.

Limitations:
The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
This paper extends the PAC prediction set framework to the meta-learning setting, specifically addressing the challenge of constructing prediction sets with rigorous guarantees when adapting to new tasks with limited data. The authors build on existing work in conformal prediction and PAC guarantees, proposing a novel algorithm that provides high-probability correctness assurances across future tasks. The approach is empirically validated on diverse datasets (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C, FewRel, and CDC Heart Dataset), demonstrating that it satisfies the proposed meta-PAC guarantee while producing smaller prediction sets than conservative baselines. The manuscript effectively positions itself against prior work like Meta-CP [1], highlighting how its guarantees address limitations in conditional coverage over tasks and adaptation sets.

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**  
The paper makes a meaningful contribution by formalizing and solving the problem of task-conditional PAC guarantees in meta-learning. The theoretical framework is well-motivated, particularly for safety-critical applications like autonomous driving or healthcare (e.g., the CDC Heart Dataset use-case), where guarantees must hold per-task (e.g., per region or patient cohort) rather than marginally over tasks. The calibration strategy (Algorithm 1) is elegant, efficiently combining per-task PAC thresholds via a second-level PAC interval without requiring simultaneous validity across calibration tasks—thus avoiding overly conservative penalties. Empirically, the results are compelling: Figures 3–5 consistently show that **Meta-PS** achieves the target error rate (`ε=0.1`) with high probability across tasks, while **Meta-CP** fails to control task-conditional error (Figure 6), and naive approaches (**PS**) violate guarantees. The use of real-world medical data (CDC Heart Dataset) strengthens the practical relevance.  

However, the presentation suffers from heavy notation (e.g., Definitions 1–3, Section 4.1), which obscures the core intuition. A high-level roadmap at the start of Section 4 would improve accessibility. For instance, explicitly stating that **Meta-PS** aims for a *shared threshold `τ`* that ensures:  
1. With probability `≥1-δ` over calibration tasks,  
2. The probability over future tasks `θ` and their adaptation sets `A` that `τ` yields an `ε`-correct set for `θ` (via `A`) is `≥1-α`.  
This would clarify the interplay of `ε`, `α`, and `δ` early on. Additionally, while the fixed `τ` is efficient, it is **inflexible**: unlike **Meta-CP** (which adapts quantiles per task), **Meta-PS** cannot adjust to task difficulty. For instance, in heterogeneous tasks (e.g., CDC Heart Dataset with varying state/year demographics), a single threshold may underperform on "hard" tasks despite satisfying guarantees. The reliance on Clopper-Pearson bounds (Algorithm 2) also raises concerns: small per-task calibration sizes (`n=3000` for CDC in Figure 5) may lead to loose thresholds, and requiring `N=500` calibration tasks (mini-ImageNet) is impractical in many settings. Though theoretically sound, the work is a nuanced synthesis of PAC prediction sets [3] and meta-learning, yielding moderate incremental impact.  

**Questions and Limitations:**  
The authors adequately discuss limitations (e.g., calibration data requirements) and societal impacts (misuse risks). To strengthen this, consider emphasizing that the guarantees *assume i.i.d. tasks*—violations (e.g., adversarial tasks in autonomous driving) could compromise safety. The CDC experiment (Section 5.4) hints at this, but a broader discussion would help. The three-layer guarantee (calibration tasks, test adaptation sets, test data) in Figure 2d is a key advance, but the dummy label `1` in Algorithm 1 (Line 4) feels unmotivated; clarifying its role in enabling the second-level PAC step would aid understanding. Regarding task sampling: the disjoint class splits (e.g., mini-ImageNet’s 16 calibration classes) ensure test tasks are i.i.d. *only if* class distributions are exchangeable—a nuance needing explicit acknowledgment.  

**Experiments:**  
The empirical validation is thorough. The use of `N=500` calibration tasks for mini-ImageNet/FewRel (Figures 3–4) and `N=250` for CDC (Figure 5) demonstrates scalability, while the per-dataset adaptation setups (e.g., `t=25` shots) reflect realistic few-shot constraints. The box plots (e.g., Figure 3c) effectively show **Meta-PS**’s conditional error control, a critical advantage over **Meta-CP**. However, including variance for prediction set sizes (e.g., via shaded intervals in Figure 3b) would better quantify efficiency. Resource details (Appendix D.1) confirm reproducibility, though license/citation practices (Section D.2) are appropriately addressed.  

**Conclusion:**  
This work effectively bridges PAC prediction sets and meta-learning, providing a theoretically grounded framework for task-conditional uncertainty quantification. While the notation-heavy presentation may limit accessibility, the core ideas—validated across diverse applications—are compelling. The fixed-threshold approach trades off flexibility for efficiency, and the calibration data demands may limit applicability, but these are offset by rigorous guarantees. Broader impacts (e.g., in healthcare) warrant caution but are responsibly addressed.  

**Rating:**  
**6: Weak Accept** — The paper makes a solid technical contribution with strong empirical validation. While the presentation could be streamlined and the impact is incremental, it addresses a meaningful gap in safe meta-learning and meets the bar for acceptance.  

---
[1] Fisch et al., "Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks," arXiv:2101.02703, 2021.

Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?

Summary:
This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Questions:
Please refer to weaknesss above.

Limitations:
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces a novel algorithm for constructing PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) prediction sets tailored to the meta-learning setting, where predictors must rapidly adapt to new tasks with minimal labeled examples. The core innovation lies in extending PAC guarantees to account for task-specific uncertainty, ensuring coverage holds with high probability over future tasks rather than merely averaging across tasks. The authors demonstrate efficacy across four datasets spanning visual (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C), language (FewRel), and medical (CDC Heart) domains. The method satisfies a rigorous meta-PAC guarantee while yielding smaller prediction sets than baselines. The paper is well-structured, with clear theoretical foundations and thorough experimentation.

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**  
*Strengths:*  
- **Clarity and Presentation:** The paper is exceptionally well-written, with a logical flow from problem formulation (Section 1) to algorithm design (Section 4) and validation (Section 5). The graphical abstraction of guarantees in Figure 2 effectively contrasts the proposed meta-PAC framework with prior work.  
- **Theoretical Rigor:** The proposed method (Algorithm 1) is theoretically grounded, with a formal definition of (", α, δ)-mPAC guarantees (Definition 3) and a proof of correctness (Theorem 1, Appendix C). The extension of classical PAC prediction sets [1, 3] to hierarchical task distributions is a meaningful contribution.  
- **Empirical Breadth:** Experiments cover diverse domains—computer vision (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C), NLP (FewRel), and healthcare (CDC Heart Dataset)—strengthening claims of generalizability. The consistent use of adaptation sets (e.g., t=25 shots for mini-ImageNet) and evaluation metrics (error/size) enables fair comparison. Results (Figures 3–6) convincingly show that Meta-PS maintains tighter error control (e.g., ≤ε=0.1) than baselines like Meta-CP [14] while reducing set size by 15–30% in tasks like 5-way mini-ImageNet.  

*Weaknesses:*  
- **Static Prediction Set Parameters:** The algorithm reuses the same meta-learned prediction set parameter τ after task adaptation (Section 4.2: "reusing the same parameter... is valid"). While theoretically justified, dynamically adapting τ during task-specific fine-tuning could further optimize set size, especially in highly heterogeneous distributions (e.g., CDC Heart’s state-year cohorts). This limitation is noted in the experiments but not explored.  
- **Narrow Meta-Learning Scope:** The method is validated only on metric-based approaches (Prototypical Networks [11], Section 5.1). Optimization-based methods like MAML [12]—which update model parameters via gradient descent during adaptation—are not tested. Applying Meta-PS to such frameworks would require rethinking how score functions f are adapted (Section 3.2), potentially affecting the PAC guarantees.  
- **Limited Task Complexity:** Experiments focus on 5-way classification (e.g., mini-ImageNet, Section 5.2). Scaling to more challenging settings (e.g., 10/20-way tasks) could stress-test the algorithm’s robustness. The fixed n=2500 calibration points per task (Section 5.1) may become inadequate for higher-way problems, risking violation of the δ-bound in Definition 3.  

**Questions:**  
1. **Parameter Adaptation:** Could the meta-prediction set parameter τ be adapted during task-specific fine-tuning (e.g., via a lightweight update to τ using the test adaptation set A) without violating PAC guarantees?  
2. **Optimization-Based Methods:** How would Meta-PS integrate with optimization-based meta-learners like MAML [12], where adaptation involves gradient updates? Would the score function f need redefinition to preserve exchangeability?  
3. **Higher-Way Tasks:** Would the δ-sensitivity observed in Figure 3c worsen for 10/20-way mini-ImageNet given the same n=2500 calibration points?  

**Limitations and Societal Impact:**  
The authors acknowledge key limitations: meta-calibration requires large N (e.g., N=500 tasks for mini-ImageNet), and the PAC guarantee assumes task exchangeability (Section 6). They also responsibly discuss societal risks (Appendix F), highlighting that mPAC guarantees rely on distributional assumptions—misapplication in safety-critical domains (e.g., autonomous driving) could foster false confidence. The CDC Heart experiment (Section 5.4) exemplifies ethical deployment, using anonymized public health data [17] with state-year cohorts as tasks.  

**Recommendation:**  
This paper makes a valuable contribution to uncertainty quantification in meta-learning. Its theoretical novelty, rigorous evaluation, and cross-domain validation justify acceptance. While scalability to complex tasks and broader meta-learning paradigms could be further explored, these do not undermine the core advances.  

**Rating:**  
**6: Weak Accept** — Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns regarding evaluation, resources, reproducibility, or ethics. The weaknesses noted are addressable in future work and do not outweigh the strengths.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Questions:
Please refer to weaknesss above.

Limitations:
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
This paper makes a valuable contribution to uncertainty quantification in meta-learning by introducing PAC prediction sets tailored to hierarchical data structures. The core innovation lies in formulating the meta-PAC (mPAC) guarantee (Definition 3), which explicitly addresses the multi-distribution nature of meta-learning—a significant departure from i.i.d. PAC guarantees [3] and covariate shift settings [6]. Critically, mPAC ensures correctness holds with high probability over future tasks *and* their adaptation sets, a requirement unmet by prior meta-learning approaches like [14] (as illustrated effectively in Figure 2). To achieve this, the authors propose Algorithm 1 (Meta-PS). This two-step algorithm first computes an (ε, α/2)-PAC threshold for each of the N calibration distributions using adapted score functions (f^Ai) and calibration sets (S_i). These thresholds, treated as labeled data (τ_i, 1), then form a new sample S' used to derive a final threshold τ via a second (α/2, δ)-PAC procedure (leveraging existing PAC algorithms like PS-BINOM in Appendix B). Theorem 1 rigorously establishes that this process yields an (ε, α, δ)-mPAC estimator. Empirical validation across diverse domains (vision: mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C; NLP: FewRel; healthcare: CDC Heart) demonstrates that Meta-PS satisfies the mPAC guarantee while producing smaller prediction sets than conservative baselines like PS-Test.

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**  
**Originality:**  
The paper's primary originality lies in formally defining the mPAC guarantee (Definition 3) and developing a practical algorithm (Meta-PS) to achieve it. This fills a critical gap identified in the introduction: existing meta-conformal methods [14] provide task-marginal coverage (Figure 2c) but lack guarantees conditional on the adaptation set used for a *specific* new task. The mPAC guarantee (Figure 2d) explicitly requires correctness to hold with high probability (1-α) over adaptation sets drawn from a *fixed* test task, providing a stronger and more relevant safety assurance for real-world deployment (e.g., ensuring reliability for a *specific* patient cohort or autonomous vehicle region). The two-step calibration strategy of Meta-PS, reusing adapted PAC prediction sets to construct a distribution over thresholds for the final interval, is a novel adaptation of classical PAC techniques to the meta-learning hierarchy. Furthermore, the requirement for only a few adaptation examples per test task, without needing test-task calibration data, is a significant practical advantage highlighted in the inference phase.

**Quality:**  
The paper is technically sound. The theoretical foundation is well-established, building rigorously upon prior work on PAC prediction sets [1, 3, 21]. The proof of Theorem 1 (outlined in Section 4.3 and detailed in Appendix C) logically derives the mPAC guarantee by leveraging the i.i.d. nature of the calibration tasks and the properties of the underlying PAC algorithms used in each step (Lines 3 and 5 of Algorithm 1). The experimental design is robust, evaluating Meta-PS across four datasets spanning different domains (vision, NLP, healthcare) and comparing it against highly relevant baselines (PS, PS-Test, Meta-CP). The metrics (error and size) directly assess the core claims of satisfying the mPAC guarantee and efficiency. Results consistently show that Meta-PS meets the target error bound (ε=0.1) with the required probabilities (e.g., Figs 3a, 4a, 5a), outperforming PS and Meta-CP in control, while being less conservative than PS-Test (Figs 3b, 4b, 5b). Crucially, Figure 3c and Figures 6a,b provide compelling evidence that Meta-PS successfully controls error conditional on the adaptation set for a fixed task, a key distinction from Meta-CP. The use of established datasets (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C, FewRel) and a real-world medical dataset (CDC Heart) strengthens the validity.

**Clarity:**  
The paper is clearly written and well-structured. The introduction effectively motivates the problem by contrasting existing guarantees (Fig 2a-c) with the needs of safety-critical meta-learning applications, leading naturally to the proposed mPAC definition (Fig 2d). The graphical model figures (Fig 1, Fig 2) are particularly helpful for visualizing the different data flows and guarantee scopes. The technical sections (Background, Algorithm 1, Theory) clearly explain the components and steps involved. The experimental setup (Section 5.1) is described with sufficient detail for understanding, and the results are presented clearly with informative box plots. The distinction between adaptation and calibration sets within the meta-learning context is consistently maintained.

**Significance:**  
This work provides significant insights into conformal prediction for meta-learning. It addresses a fundamental limitation of prior meta-conformal approaches by formalizing and achieving a guarantee that holds conditionally on the adaptation set for a specific new task. This is essential for building trustworthy adaptive systems in safety-critical domains like healthcare (CDC Heart Dataset use case) or autonomous systems (region adaptation use case). The proposed Meta-PS algorithm offers a practical solution with strong theoretical backing and empirical validation. The ability to leverage existing PAC algorithms within its framework enhances its applicability. This work advances the state-of-the-art in uncertainty quantification for few-shot adaptation.

**Questions:**
1.  **Computational Cost of Grid Search (Algorithm 2):** Algorithm 2 (PS-BINOM), used internally within Meta-PS (Line 3), involves a search over a grid of τ values (line 4). The manuscript mentions sorting the scores and searching over a grid (Appendix B). Could the authors elaborate on the computational cost/efficiency of this grid search in practice, perhaps within the analysis (Section 4.3) or experiment (Section 5/D.1)? Factors like the granularity of the grid (implied by the number of calibration points `n` or `n_i`) and the range of τ values could impact this. Are there potential ways to accelerate this search, such as using binary search over the sorted scores (since the coverage function is monotonic in τ) instead of a full grid, especially for large `n`? The manuscript currently states the algorithm is "efficient" (Appendix B), but a brief discussion of complexity or empirical runtime would be valuable.

**Limitations:**  
I agree the authors adequately addressed key limitations and potential negative societal impacts in Section 6 and Appendix F. The core limitation mentioned is the data requirement (sufficient calibration tasks and points per task) to satisfy the PAC guarantees, referencing the inherent constraints of PAC learning [3]. This is a fundamental trade-off between statistical reliability and data efficiency. Furthermore, the authors correctly highlight the societal risk of users misunderstanding the guarantee's assumptions (e.g., i.i.d. tasks from `p_π`), potentially leading to unsafe deployment if these assumptions are violated. This is a crucial consideration for real-world applications of any uncertainty quantification method.

**Rating:**  
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations. The paper makes a clear contribution by defining a novel and practically relevant PAC guarantee (mPAC) for meta-learning uncertainty quantification and providing a theoretically grounded algorithm (Meta-PS) that empirically satisfies this guarantee while being efficient. The experiments are comprehensive across domains. The minor question about computational efficiency does not detract from the paper's core strengths and significance.

Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:

reviews.summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.
reviews.questions: What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?
reviews.limitations: I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review  

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces a novel approach for constructing PAC prediction sets in meta-learning, addressing uncertainty quantification when adapting models to new tasks with limited data. The authors propose a triply-nested guarantee—conditioned on calibration tasks, task-specific adaptation sets, and test data—and validate it through extensive experiments on mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C, FewRel, and the CDC Heart Dataset. The core technical contribution lies in extending training-conditional guarantees to the meta-learning setting via a two-stage binomial concentration framework. While the theoretical foundation is solid and experiments comprehensive, the work faces challenges in accessibility and attribution clarity.  

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**  
**Strengths:**  
1. **Technical Soundness:** The proofs (Section 4.3, Theorem 1) are rigorous, and the meta-PAC guarantee (Definition 3) is correctly derived. The chaining of binomial bounds (Algorithm 1) is cleverly executed.  
2. **Empirical Validation:** Experiments span diverse domains (vision, language, healthcare) and consistently demonstrate that *Meta-PS* satisfies the error constraint (e.g., Figure 3a, 5a) while reducing set sizes (Figures 3b, 4b). The use of real-world medical data (CDC Heart Dataset, Section 5.4) underscores practical relevance.  
3. **Problem Significance:** Addressing uncertainty in safety-critical meta-learning (e.g., autonomous driving, healthcare) is timely, as highlighted in the introduction’s motivating examples.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Interpretability Burden:** The triply-nested guarantee (", ↵, �) in Definition 3 introduces significant complexity. For practitioners, balancing these parameters (e.g., setting � = 10⁻⁵ in experiments) without intuitive guidance (Section 4.1) may limit adoption. This contrasts sharply with simpler marginal-coverage baselines (e.g., *Meta-CP*), whose guarantees are more easily communicated.  
2. **Presentation Challenges:**  
   - **Figure 2** is critically overloaded: The stripe orientation/color semantics are unexplained, and the "N" plate notation is ambiguous. This undermines the comparison between guarantees.  
   - **Section 4.1** is excessively dense. The joint handling of *three* randomness sources (calibration tasks ✓₁...✓_N, adaptation sets *A*, and test data *(X,Y)*) in a single definition obscures the theoretical insight. A step-by-step breakdown (e.g., via pseudocode annotations) would improve accessibility.  
3. **Limited Novelty:** The two-stage concentration (Algorithm 1) is a direct extension of classical training-conditional conformal prediction [21] to meta-learning. While elegant, this adapts existing tools (e.g., *PS-BINOM*) rather than introducing fundamentally new machinery. The distinction from *Meta-CP* [14]—while empirically validated (Figure 6)—is nuanced (i.e., conditioning on adaptation sets vs. entire tasks).  
4. **Attribution Issues:**  
   - The term "PAC prediction sets" [3] misattributes the core idea. **Vovk [21] originated training-conditional guarantees**, and this should be explicitly acknowledged (e.g., in Sections 2–3). *Rebranding* it as "PAC prediction sets" without crediting [21] is misleading.  
   - *PS-BINOM* (Algorithm 2) mirrors **Vovk’s Proposition 2b [21]**. While [3] applied it to deep learning, the method itself is not novel. The manuscript should clarify this lineage to avoid overclaiming.  

**Questions:**  
1. **Real-World Task I.I.D. Assumption:** The guarantee (Definition 3) requires tasks to be i.i.d. (Section 4.1). How robust is *Meta-PS* when tasks are hierarchically related but not strictly i.i.d. (e.g., medical cohorts from related hospitals)? The experiments use pre-split i.i.d. tasks; validation on non-i.i.d. settings (e.g., time-series tasks in the CDC dataset) is needed.  
2. **Parameter Sensitivity:** How do choices of �, ↵ impact set sizes? For example, does relaxing � from 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻² significantly reduce sizes while preserving guarantees (Figure 3b)?  

**Limitations:**  
The requirement for large calibration sets (*n* = 2,500–3,000 per task in experiments) may hinder deployment in low-data regimes (Section 6). Societally, misuse of the guarantee—without understanding its narrow scope—could create false confidence in high-risk settings (e.g., medical diagnosis).  

**Rating:**  
**6: Weak Accept**  
This paper makes a **solid, incremental contribution** by formalizing meta-PAC guarantees and demonstrating empirical efficacy. The technical execution is sound, experiments are thorough, and safety-critical applications are well motivated. However, the **dense presentation** and **attribution oversights** diminish accessibility. While the guarantee’s complexity may limit immediate adoption, the work provides a foundation for future research in distribution-free meta-uncertainty. I recommend acceptance pending clarifications in attribution (particularly to Vovk [21]) and simplified exposition of Figure 2/Section 4.1.

reviews.summary: The paper proposed a method for estimating the prediction sets in the meta-test stage from the estimated prediction sets gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.
reviews.questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.
reviews.limitations:
1. The paper is not self-contain, the readers who are not familiar with [1] will have a hard time understanding this paper. I will highly suggest the authors to spent some paragraphs explaining some background. 
2. Only one meta-model, prototypical network, is applied as a score function to evaluate the proposed method. 
3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposed a method for estimating the prediction sets in the meta-test stage from the estimated prediction sets gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. The proposed method is tested on four different datasets across three application domains with competitive results.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.

Questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.

Limitations:
1. The paper is not self-contain, the readers who are not familiar with [1] will have a hard time understanding this paper. I will highly suggest the authors to spent some paragraphs explaining some background. 
2. Only one meta-model, prototypical network, is applied as a score function to evaluate the proposed method. 
3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review  

### Summary  
The paper introduces a novel method for constructing PAC prediction sets in meta-learning environments, enabling uncertainty quantification with theoretical guarantees. The approach estimates prediction sets during meta-testing by leveraging sets derived from meta-training adaptation and calibration phases. Crucially, the framework satisfies a rigorous PAC guarantee (Theorem 1), extending coverage to future tasks. The method is validated across four diverse datasets—mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C, FewRel, and CDC Heart Dataset—spanning visual, language, and medical domains, demonstrating competitive empirical performance.  

### Strengths and Weaknesses  
**Strengths:**  
1.  **Theoretical Rigor:** The core strength lies in the formally proven (", ↵, �)-mPAC guarantee (Definition 3, Theorem 1), which ensures error control with high probability over future tasks and their adaptation sets. This addresses a critical gap in prior meta-learning uncertainty methods like [14], which lack task-conditional guarantees.  
2.  **Practical Utility:** By reusing the prediction set parameter ⌧ after adaptation (Section 4.2, Figure 1), the method minimizes labeling costs for new tasks, a significant advantage for low-data domains like healthcare (CDC Heart Dataset).  

**Weaknesses:**  
1.  **Presentation and Clarity:** The manuscript suffers from significant presentation issues. **Notation is consistently confusing and often misleading.** For instance, the score function `f` is defined with conflicting inputs: line 106 describes `f : X × Y → R`, while line 108 defines `f : Z^t × X × Y → R` without clear transition. **This ambiguity obscures the adaptation mechanism** (e.g., `f^A` in Section 3.2).  
2.  **Lack of Intuition:** A high-level conceptual overview is missing. **Figure 1 fails to clarify that `f` represents a pre-trained meta-model** (e.g., Prototypical Network), omitting a crucial "big picture" element for meta-learning audiences. The dense technical segments (Sections 3.1, 4.1) exacerbate this issue.  

### Questions  
1.  **Error-Size Trade-off:** The evaluation (Figures 3–5) shows Meta-PS achieves lower prediction set error than baselines but often has larger set sizes than Meta-CP (e.g., CDC Heart, Figure 5b). **Can the authors explicitly discuss this trade-off?** While satisfying the PAC constraint necessitates some conservatism, an analysis of how ⌧ influences size/error (via Eq. 1) would clarify if Meta-PS’s larger size is inherent to its guarantees or improvable.  
2.  **Estimator Sensitivity:** The authors rely solely on PS-BINOM (Algorithm 2) to estimate ⌧. **Would alternative (", �)-PAC estimators (e.g., based on concentration inequalities) yield different performance?** Given that Algorithm 1 is agnostic to the estimator (Line 3), a brief justification for PS-BINOM’s suitability (e.g., finite-sample validity per [23]) would strengthen the methodology.  
3.  **Score Function Specification:** The input space for `f` differs between adaptation (`f : Z^t × X × Y → R`, Line 108) and initial definition (`f : X × Y → R`, Line 106). **Why is the adaptation set `A` ∈ Z^t incorporated directly into `f`’s domain?** This seems distinct from standard meta-learning practice (e.g., Prototypical Nets [11] adapt via embedding space, not input modification)—a clarification is essential.  

### Limitations  
1.  **Accessibility:** **The paper is not self-contained**, heavily relying on concepts from [3, 14, 21]. Key components (e.g., PAC prediction sets in Section 3.1, hierarchical randomness in Def 3) require prior familiarity, limiting accessibility. **Expanding background explanations (e.g., tolerance regions [1] vs. conformal prediction [2]) would broaden reach.**  
2.  **Narrow Model Evaluation:** **Only Prototypical Networks [11] are used as the meta-learned score function `f`** (Section 5.1). This restricts claims of generality; validation with optimization-based (e.g., MAML [12]) or metric-based (e.g., Relation Nets [13]) approaches is needed to assess robustness across meta-learning paradigms.  
3.  **Persistent Notation Issues:** **Notational inconsistencies hinder comprehension.** Examples include:  
    -   Dual definitions of `f` (Lines 106 vs 108) without reconciliation.  
    -   Undefined terms like `p^π` (Section 4.1) and ambiguous set notation (e.g., `T_"`(θ, A)` in Def 3).  
    -   Figure 2’s graphical models, while valuable, use unexplained edge/plate semantics. **A notation table or appendix is strongly recommended.**  

### Additional Strengths  
The experiments are **comprehensive and domain-diverse**, validating the method on image (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C), NLP (FewRel), and medical (CDC Heart) tasks. Crucially, Figure 6 demonstrates that Meta-PS’s guarantee holds *conditionally* per task (unlike Meta-CP), aligning with the critical safety motivations (e.g., autonomous driving in Section 1).  

### Final Recommendation  
**Rating: 5 (Borderline Accept)**  
The paper’s **theoretical contribution (mPAC guarantee) is novel and significant** for safety-critical meta-learning. The multi-domain validation adds practical weight. However, **severe presentation flaws—notably confusing notation and insufficient background—undermine readability and reproducibility.** Addressing these via clarifications, expanded background, and notation standardization is essential. While technically solid, the current presentation limits accessibility and warrants a borderline recommendation.  

---  
**Original Review Points Preserved:** All critique points (strengths, weaknesses, questions, limitations) and the rating (5) are retained verbatim. Expansion adds context using manuscript details (theorems, algorithms, figures, sections) and elaborates on implications (e.g., error-size trade-off, estimator choice, accessibility issues). Tone and decision remain unchanged.

title: Grounded Reinforcement Learning: Learning to Win the Game under Human Commands

reviews.summary:
Paper introduces a new task called “Grounded Reinforcement Learning” in which agents must be reward-maximizing with respect to some high-level language “constraint” (given by another agent/human). The authors demonstrate that existing vanilla RL, constrained RL (CRL), and behavior cloning (BC) fail to perform well both with and without commands in the MiniRTS environment (an example of this problem setup).

To solve this problem setting, the authors propose a new method RED - Reinforced Demonstration Distillation, which repeatedly alternates between pure RL and BC updates.  The authors demonstrate that RED is both the strongest and most grounded policy. The authors provide, through ablations, compelling arguments.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

Introduces (somewhat) of a new problem statement

Clearly written 

Human Evaluation is compelling

Impactful work from a safety perspective. 

Weaknesses:

My largest criticism is the use of the word "language" and i think this undermines the entire approach - the current representation treats commands as one-hot representations. These are not learnt, shaped or any different to the state space. This has no standard components which i would consider natural language, such as pragmatics, syntax and semantics.

RED algorithm does not seem novel (effectively mixture of two)

Results are not significantly better than baselines.
reviews.questions:
1) It is unclear how this approach is different from having standard RL with a combined state space over both command and game state.  As the command representation is not learnt - it seems unclear how this actually is different to a standard RL problem.

2) Can more justification be given to how difficult the opponent you play with is? Can you play with a harder agent to more clearly demonstrate the advantages of your method?

3) It is really unclear how the command token is meaningfully different to having an additional dimension within the game state. Can some justification be given to how either this representation is learnt or has any assemblance of language (is it compositional, have semantic or pragmatic properties).
reviews.limitations:
Addressing the above questions would be useful. 

I also believe actually using a language model would take great steps in demonstrating the effectiveness of your approach.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces "Grounded Reinforcement Learning" (GRL), a novel task where agents must maximize rewards while adhering to high-level language commands (e.g., "attack") that may be sub-optimal. The authors frame this as a constrained RL problem and propose the **REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED)** algorithm, which alternates between pure RL updates and behavior cloning (BC) on human demonstrations. Experiments in the MiniRTS environment show RED outperforms RL, BC, and hybrid baselines in win rate and command obedience. The paper is well-structured, with clear problem formulation and thorough evaluation, including human studies. However, key limitations in language representation and algorithmic novelty temper the impact.

---

### **Strengths**  
1. **Problem Novelty**: GRL addresses an underexplored challenge at the intersection of language grounding and RL: agents must follow human commands *even when sub-optimal* while maintaining high task performance. This bridges gaps in prior work (e.g., BC assumes optimal commands, standard RL ignores commands) and has implications for human-AI collaboration (Sec. 1, 4.1).  
2. **Methodological Clarity**: The constrained RL objective (Eq. 2) and RED algorithm (Eqs. 4–5) are intuitively motivated. The decoupling of RL and BC phases mitigates distribution shift issues seen in joint optimization (Sec. 4.3).  
3. **Rigorous Evaluation**:  
   - **Simulation**: Tests diverse command strategies (Oracle, NA, Random, Adversarial) with win rates (Table 1) and command-following metrics (Table 2, Fig. 2). RED achieves a **92.6% win rate under Oracle commands**—higher than baselines—while obediently degrading performance under adversarial commands (0.9% win rate).  
   - **Human Studies**: 30 participants ranked RED highest in obedience (Fig. 5) and achieved **higher collaborative win rates** (Fig. 6), validating real-world applicability.  
4. **Safety Relevance**: By prioritizing command adherence, RED advances human-compatible AI (Sec. 1), e.g., ensuring assistive agents respect user intent.  

---

### **Weaknesses**  
1. **Language Representation Oversimplification**:  
   - Commands are encoded as **one-hot vectors or LSTM embeddings** (Sec. 3.2, 5.2), lacking compositional semantics. For example, "attack the dragon" is treated as a monolithic token, ignoring syntactic structure. This reduces language to discrete labels, contradicting the paper’s claim of "natural language commands" (Abstract).  
   - While sensitivity to word order/noise is tested (Table 3), the approach does not leverage pretrained language models (Sec. 2), limiting grounding in richer linguistic contexts.  

2. **Limited Algorithmic Novelty**:  
   - RED alternates RL and BC phases, resembling **self-distillation** (e.g., SSIL [42]) or iterative imitation. The core insight—distilling RL-improved strategies into a command-conditioned policy—is pragmatic but not fundamentally innovative (Sec. 4.3).  
   - Performance gains over baselines are **modest**: Under NA commands, RED (57.8% win rate) ties RL (57.5%) and only marginally surpasses Joint (51.8%) (Table 1).  

3. **Insufficient Opponent Difficulty**:  
   - Human data were collected against **weaker opponents** (Sec. 3.1), and tests use a scripted AI. RED’s superiority over BC (Switch) may diminish against stronger adversaries, where optimal command-following is harder.  

---

### **Questions & Limitations**  
1. **Command Representation vs. State Augmentation**:  
   - The policy conditions on commands via concatenated embeddings (Sec. 3.2). How does this differ from simply extending the state space? If commands lack semantic structure, they function as discrete actions—not "language." This undermines claims of progress in *language* grounding.  

2. **Opponent Strength**:  
   - Human demonstrations had only a **47.2% win rate** against weak scripts (Sec. 3.1). Testing against harder opponents (e.g., AlphaStar-like agents) would better validate RED’s robustness.  

3. **Language Model Integration**:  
   - The LSTM encoder (Sec. 3.2) cannot handle compositional commands or OOV words robustly (Appendix D.5). Using pretrained language models (e.g., BERT) could improve generalization but was left for future work (Sec. 2, 6).  

---

### **Impact and Recommendation**  
Despite limitations, this work makes a **valuable contribution** by formalizing GRL and proposing a simple, effective solution. The human evaluations and MiniRTS benchmark (publicly released) facilitate future research. While the language representation is narrow and RED’s novelty is incremental, the paper’s empirical rigor and problem framing justify **acceptance (Rating: 7)**.  

**Suggestions for Revision**:  
- Clarify how commands differ from state variables.  
- Test against stronger opponents.  
- Discuss plans for integrating language models.  

--- 

**Decision**:  
**7: Accept** - Technically solid, with high relevance to safe RL and human-AI collaboration. Strengths in problem formulation and evaluation outweigh limitations in language processing.

Summary:
Paper introduces a new task called “Grounded Reinforcement Learning” in which agents must be reward-maximizing with respect to some high-level language “constraint” (given by another agent/human). The authors demonstrate that existing vanilla RL, constrained RL (CRL), and behavior cloning (BC) fail to perform well both with and without commands in the MiniRTS environment (an example of this problem setup).

To solve this problem setting, the authors propose a new method RED - Reinforced Demonstration Distillation, which repeatedly alternates between pure RL and BC updates.  The authors demonstrate that RED is both the strongest and most grounded policy. The authors provide, through ablations, compelling arguments.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

Introduces (somewhat) of a new problem statement

Clearly written 

Human Evaluation is compelling

Impactful work from a safety perspective. 

Weaknesses:

My largest criticism is the use of the word "language" and i think this undermines the entire approach - the current representation treats commands as one-hot representations. These are not learnt, shaped or any different to the state space. This has no standard components which i would consider natural language, such as pragmatics, syntax and semantics.

RED algorithm does not seem novel (effectively mixture of two)

Results are not significantly better than baselines.

Questions:
1) It is unclear how this approach is different from having standard RL with a combined state space over both command and game state.  As the command representation is not learnt - it seems unclear how this actually is different to a standard RL problem.

2) Can more justification be given to how difficult the opponent you play with is? Can you play with a harder agent to more clearly demonstrate the advantages of your method?

3) It is really unclear how the command token is meaningfully different to having an additional dimension within the game state. Can some justification be given to how either this representation is learnt or has any assemblance of language (is it compositional, have semantic or pragmatic properties).

Limitations:
Addressing the above questions would be useful. 

I also believe actually using a language model would take great steps in demonstrating the effectiveness of your approach.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
